definition: A formula that combines five financial ratios to flag the risk of bankruptcy within two years.
A way for two pieces of software to send data to each other, like a waiter for code.
The average dollar amount a stock moves per day, over the last two weeks.
A snapshot of what a company owns, what it owes, and what is left for shareholders.
A long stretch when prices have fallen 20% or more from a recent peak.
The father of value investing, author of The Intelligent Investor, teacher of Warren Buffett.
A measure of how much a stock tends to move relative to the overall market. Above 1 is more.
The bid is the highest price a buyer will pay; the ask is the lowest price a seller will accept.
A moving average with upper and lower lines showing the recent high and low of normal price.
The net assets of a company on its balance sheet, equity owed to shareholders.
A long stretch when prices are generally rising and investors feel confident.
A strategy of buying investments and keeping them for years, ignoring short-term noise.
The currency of Canada, often the reporting currency for Altier Edge portfolios and prices.
The profit you make when you sell an investment for more than you paid for it.
The portion of a profit from selling an investment that Canadian tax treats as taxable income.
A report showing the actual cash moving in and out of a business over a period.
Buffett's partner; champion of buying wonderful businesses at fair prices, not the reverse.
The set of businesses you actually understand well enough to judge the value of.
Interest that earns interest, so growth accelerates over time as earlier gains pile up.
An investor willing to buy what others are selling and sell what others are buying.
A drop of 10% to 20% from a recent peak, a normal and frequent part of markets.
The original price you paid per share, used to calculate profit or loss when you sell.
The Canadian federal agency that administers tax law, including investment account rules.
A live stream of prices or news delivered by a vendor into an app or model.
How many dollars the company owes for every dollar of shareholder money on the books.
Spreading your money across different investments so one bad position does not sink the portfolio.
A cash payment a company sends to shareholders, usually out of its profits.
The annual dividend as a percentage of the current share price. 3% to 6% is meaningful.
A company's profit, usually reported every three months and once a year in detail.
Earnings before interest, taxes, depreciation, and amortization, a rough operating profit measure.
A dividend from a Canadian public company taxed more favourably than interest income.
An average of recent prices that weights newer prices more heavily than older ones.
The full price tag of a business: market value plus debt minus cash on hand.
The company's profit divided by the number of shares, the building block of the P/E ratio.
A basket of investments that trades on an exchange like a single stock, usually low-cost.
The full price of the business, including debt, against its core operating earnings.
A regulated marketplace where buyers and sellers trade stocks, like the TSX or Nasdaq.
An academic model explaining returns with market, size, value, profitability, and investment factors.
A Canadian account that combines tax-deductible savings with tax-free withdrawal for a first home.
Likely support and resistance levels after a big move, based on percentage pullbacks like 38% and 62%.
The cash the business throws off each year, compared to its market value. Higher is better.
Studying a company's financial statements and business to estimate its real value.
A rough upper-bound fair price, calculated from earnings and book value per share.
The share of each sales dollar left after the direct cost of producing the goods or service.
A report showing revenue, costs, and profit over a period, usually a quarter or year.
A fund that holds the same investments as a market index, like the S&P 500 or TSX 60.
What a business is really worth based on its future cash, not today's share price.
When a private company first sells shares to the public to raise money on a stock exchange.
A simple text format for storing and sending data, easy for both humans and computers to read.
Borrowing money to amplify an investment, which can magnify both gains and losses.
An order to buy or sell only at a price you specify or better, not at the current market price.
How easily an investment can be bought or sold without moving the price much.
When several forces line up in the same direction, the combined result is far more than the sum.
A trend indicator that shows whether short-term price is pulling away from long-term price.
Buying well below what something is worth, so being wrong by a little still leaves a profit.
The total value of a company, calculated as share price multiplied by shares outstanding.
An order to buy or sell immediately at the best price currently available.
A durable edge that protects a business from competitors, like a brand or a cost advantage.
The tendency of stocks that have risen recently to keep rising for a while longer.
Benjamin Graham's metaphor for the market: an emotional manic friend offering prices daily.
A pooled investment managed by a professional, priced once a day rather than trading live.
A running tally of whether more shares trade on up-days or down-days, signaling real demand.
The running total of how much a position or portfolio has made or lost over a period.
The share price compared to the net assets the company owns per share. Below 1 is a discount.
How many dollars you pay for each dollar of a company's yearly profit. Lower usually means cheaper.
A fake list of holdings used to practise decisions without risking actual cash.
A simulated buy or sell that tracks real prices but uses no real money.
The share of profits paid out as dividends. Above 80% may not be sustainable.
An irreversible hit to your invested money, the risk that matters more than price swings.
A nine-point yes/no checklist of profitability, leverage, and operating health. 8 or 9 is strong.
Deciding how much of your money to put into one investment, based on risk.
What is left of revenue after all the costs, taxes, and interest are paid.
A scoring overlay that rewards profitability, safety, and stable earnings.
An investor who prioritizes strong businesses and durable advantages over cheap prices.
How much profit the company makes for each dollar of shareholder equity. Above 15% is strong.
The total money a company brings in from sales before any costs are deducted.
The compound annual growth rate of sales over the last three years. Above 10% is fast.
How much short-term loss you can stomach without abandoning your long-term plan.
A Canadian retirement account where contributions are tax-deductible and growth is tax-deferred.
A 0-to-100 score showing how fast and how far a stock's price has moved recently.
A group of companies in the same industry, like energy, banks, or technology.
The Canadian public database where companies file continuous disclosure documents.
One unit of ownership in a company, identical in rights to every other share of the same class.
Borrowing shares to sell now, hoping to buy them back later at a lower price.
The gap between the bid and the ask price, often a rough cost of trading.
A 0-to-100 score showing where today's close sits inside the recent high-low range.
A share of ownership in a company, entitling you to a claim on its profits and votes.
An order to sell a stock automatically if it falls to a price you set, to limit a loss.
A Canadian account where growth and withdrawals are tax-free, up to a yearly contribution room.
A short code, like AAPL or SHOP, used to identify a stock on an exchange.
Canada's main stock exchange, where most large Canadian companies list their shares.
A Canadian exchange for smaller and earlier-stage resource and growth companies.
Buying stocks for less than they are worth, then waiting for the market to agree.
How much a price swings around over time, higher means wilder and riskier.
The number of shares traded in a given day, showing how actively a stock is changing hands.
The day's average trading price, weighted by how many shares traded at each price.
Long-time investor known for buying great businesses at fair prices and holding for decades.
An automatic message one app sends another when something happens, like a price crossing a threshold.
A -100 to 0 score showing where today's close sits in the recent high-low range.
The income from an investment over a year, expressed as a percentage of its price.
